content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What is the most obscure 90s movie you would recommend?

**Score:** 128
**Body:** For example: I would recommend **The Music of Chance (1993)** James Spader and Mandy Patinkin try to hustle two eccentric millionaires in a high stakes poker game. It's a little-seen low budget indie movie based on an excellent novel by Paul Auster. Also starring M Emmet Walsh, Chris Penn and Charles Durning.

Bonus recommendation: **Deceiver (1997)** A flawed but decent thriller starring Tim Roth as a weirdo who may or may not be a serial killer. Chris Penn and Michael Rooker are the cops who are tasked with interrogating him. It features an excellent cameo from Michael Parks as a psychologist who warns them about Tim Roth's propensity for violence.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Zero Effect is really fun. Bill Pullman and Ben Stiller. Private detective mystery comedy. 


Also, maybe not that obscure but Sneakers is *fantastic*. I mean, just look at the cast. I’m shocked at how few ratings it has on IMDB.
2. Suicide Kings
3. Things to do in Denver when you’re dead
4. Go. 

Great little movie that very accurately portraits the Los Angeles rave scene in the 90’s through an ensemble cast of weirdos and partiers.
5. Rosencrantz and Guildenstern Are Dead
6. Flirting With Disaster - 1996
7. The Spanish Prisoner- Steve Martin and Campbell Scott. Not a comedy.
8. Devil In A Blue Dress(1995)
9. Surviving the game: Gary busey and Rutger Hauer hunt Ice-T for sport
10. Red Rock West

## 2. Best dramatized movies about a famous person

**Score:** 41
**Body:** What are the best movies about a celebrity’s life or career? I’m looking for dramatized movies not documentaries. 

Examples of the kind of movie I’m looking for are Air,  42, Ferrari, Oppenheimer, and The Founder.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Amadeus 
2. Not a joke: Abraham Lincoln: Vampire Hunter (2012).   Trust me.... this movie goes hard.
3. Temple Grandin

Sweet Dreams

A Coal Miners Daughter
  - Temple Grandin is amazing!
4. American Splendor

Straight Outta Compton

John Adams (a limited series, not a movie)

And that’s just Paul Giamatti.
5. King Richard - about Venus and Serena Williams and their father who encouraged them to play tennis growing up 

Goodfellas - based on the true story of Henry Hill and a fantastic movie 

First Man - about Neil Armstrong’s journey into space 

Hidden Figures - about the Black women who enabled many of NASA’s successes (maybe not famous though, because the whole point is that these women were overlooked despite the huge impact they had on the space program) 

On the Basis of Sex - about Ruth Bader Ginsburg’s early career 

House of Gucci - about the people who started the Gucci fashion brand
6. Weird: The Al Yankovic Story
7. \*\*The Social Network\*\* is probably my go-to for this kind of thing - Fincher absolutely nailed the whole Facebook origin story drama
8. A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood (2019)
9. "Ed Wood"  is really fantastic. Incredible cast and makes you really root for him

## 3. Girlie friendship movies

**Score:** 3
**Body:** My all time fav movie is Aquamarine, i also love now and then (1995), the sisterhood of the travelling pants, steel magnolias, etc. So just looking for more girlie friendship movies! Bonus points if they're summer movies but not a requirement !

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Romy and Michelle High School Reunion
2. While it’s not the central plot, 9 to 5 has a strong trio friendship that develops (and is also one of the funniest movies I’ve ever seen)
3. Now and Then

Nevermind - I didn’t see it already listed by OP. Capitalizing definitely aides reading.
4. About Last Night

Foxfire

Thelma & Louise

Bridesmaids
5. Divine Secrets of the Ya-Ya Sisterhood
6. Fried Green Tomatoes 

Beaches

Waiting to Exhale
7. The craft (1996)

The craft : legacy (2020)

Girl, interrupted (1999)

Sunny (2011) korean movie
8. Kamikaze Girls
9. Our Time (1974)
10. Enchanted April

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Fiancé and I watched Anaconda what was John Voight doing in this movie?

**Score:** 748
**Body:** He claims he was from Paraguay with his accent raging from fake Hispanic, Eastern European, American accent while looking like a guy that steals copper wire for meth. Man, since I was a kid I loved this movie, but its all bad, like an entertaining bad.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Him all breathing hard in an accent somehow and looking at JLo 😂

I always forget that Owen Wilson was in that too.
Such a stupidly funny movie.
  - Owen Wilson becoming the second bad guy and failing was hilarious and man, as someone that owned snakes, like Pythons and Boas, this snake sure can eat. I was feeding mine like every 2 or 3 weeks.
2. Acting his fuckin ass off
  - Maybe they went to film and just happened to run into Jon Voight hiding out in South America for Jon Voight reasons.
3. His BEST
4. Getting a paycheck
5. Paying off his IRS bill, that's what.

See: Guiness, Sir Alec
6. Cocaine
7. What it always got me is the “wink” when he’s being eaten by the Anaconda. Top acting there
8. Needed money to buy a Le Baron..

## 2. Stellan Skarsgård Wins Golden Globe for Best Supporting Actor ‘Sentimental Value,’ Says ‘Cinema Should Be Seen in Cinemas’

**Score:** 214

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Man, I did not realize how anti-theatre this sub was. Very bizarre for a movie loving sub.
  - People watch a shitty blockbuster movie where it is full of kids and teenagers and are surprised people act uncivilised.

As someone who saw Secret Agent, Sentimental Value, Sorry Baby, The Last Viking, Angels Egg, Die My Love, Bugonia, One Battle After another in the cinema recently, no one said a thing. No one moved.
    - IDK I got a season pass at Alamo Draft House and its been a saving grace for me. I dont mean to plug but it saves you money. I wfh and am VERY poor, but this allows me to do something for myself and in a sense go out and socialize. I love going to the movies. And yeah sometimes people suck, but not always. I can understand how what hes saying can be read as tonedeaf but i also kind of get it. I also dont have kids, so i need not worry there.  TBH I dont know how yall even afford kids let alone taking them anywhere.
  - Bunch of neckbeards
2. Great actor and wonderful human
3. Still in character I see
4. Deep
5. I find it surprising that despite actors saying cinema should be seen in cinemas, they don’t have the slightest clues as to why less ppl are going to the theaters. Or maybe they do, but these rich folks dont care about us, they just care more about taking our money
  - There is totally an aspect of self interest to it. It's in their best financial interest for us to go to the theatre.
  - Jesus Christ. He is addressing Netflix. Not you. Also he is Swedish and the cinemas and concessions are not super pricey

## 3. Favorite double agent in a movie? I'll start Snape in Harry Potter

**Score:** 150

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Damon in the departed is a gloriously weasely character
2. Alan Rickman really brought a sympathetic side to Snape that isn't really there... He didn't care about doing the right thing in the book. He wanted Lily, not equality, and his motives were always selfish in nature.
  - I just rewached the movies and tbh, i got that from the movies aswell. I think the sympathy people feel for him comes mainly from his last scenes, but dven then hes pretty much just saying he wants to Fk harry’s dead mom
    - I think that's what they mean though. He's only sympathetic because of how Alan Rickman portrayed him in his last moments. In the book, we didn't get that as much.
3. Not a movie but Ocelot in Metal Gear Solid.
His agendas agenda has an agenda.
  - Only loyal to Big Boss
4. Verbal Kint/Keyser Söze ("The Usual Suspects")
  - I get what you mean, but he wasn’t an agent really. He didn’t have a goal other than lying to get himself out of trouble
5. Cypher
6. Sam in Ronin.

DeNiro at his best portraying a mercenary for hire then actually being a CIA agent

## 4. That one cheese ball of a movie you hate to admit you like

**Score:** 125
**Body:** Over the years I’ve definitely seen my fair share of cheesy movies. I’m not sure what’s at the top of your list, but for me, The Last Dragon is at the top of the list of cheesy movies I actually like.

I saw this movie in the theaters back when it came out. I was a kid at that time, and absolutely loved everything about it. 

About 5 years ago, I watched this movie for the first time as an adult. It was in that moment that I realized how truly cheesy it all was.

Maybe it was the nostalgia of it all, but did I still enjoy it? Sho’Nuff I did!

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hate to admit!? This is a cult classic!
  - Yeah wtf is OP on about
  - Sho Nuff!
2. The Golden Child with Eddie Murphy. 

Maybe because it was one of the few movies we could rent at the corner store when I was a kid.
3. This movie is gold. SHO NUFF
  - Coolest villain name EVER!
4. Why? It's a fun movie. I love it!
5. “Directa yo feetza to Daddy Gerene’s Pizza”
6. Julius Carry is amazingly over the top as Sho 'Nuff. Final fight scene is awesome too.
7. It is a great fun movie, and makes a good double bill with 'Remo: Unarmed and Dangerous'

## 5. Watching Harry and the Hendersons for the first time since the early 90s, does it hold up?

**Score:** 120

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Every John Lithgow performance stands the test of time, regardless of the overall quality of the whole product. It's worth watching for him alone.
  - George Hen?…
    - Yes, my name is George Hen.
2. I like to watch it in the middle of Dexter season 4 and pretend it’s the Trinity Killer’s flashback
3. Uhhhh YES. The slap scene destroyed me as a kid and still does now. The movie has no right being as amazing as it is.
  - Did we just become best friends?
    - It's a genuinely great movie and people who try to pretend otherwise are just lying to themselves. You and me know what's up.
4. When there were like 3 family movies total it was pretty good
5. “Is that Harry and the Hendersons?”

“Oh so you’ve seen it”

“This is my life Jack”
6. Random fact, Kevin Peter Hall played Harry (big foot) and was also the Predator in the original 1987 arnie film

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Bi Gan’s *Resurrection* (2025) Feels Like a Self-Parody and the Death of His Own Language

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I’d like to preface this by saying that I am a huge Bi Gan fan; *Kaili Blues*, in particular, is one of my all-time favorite films, one I’ve rewatched countless times. I hold *Long Day’s Journey Into Night* in similarly high regard. Watching both feels like a journey akin to Tarkovsky’s *Stalker*.

That being said, this film felt like a major letdown. It follows the same structural rubric as his previous work: Person X searching for Person Y, an ethereal, dreamlike journey, deliberate opacity, culminating in X finding Y, either in or with an extended long take. At this point, that structure feels unoriginal and offers little that is formally or emotionally new. What once felt revelatory now feels mannered. 

The stylization, especially at the beginning and near the end before the credits, felt distasteful. I understand the intention as an anthology of cinema’s history, but the execution came off as tacky and inert, lacking the rawness that defined his earlier films. The heavy color grading and overt aestheticization drained the images of their gravity. At times, it felt like a Netflix pastiche rather than the raw, lived dreamscape Bi Gan once curated in his previous works.

Narratively, the film is incoherent. Ambiguity in *Kaili Blues* and *Long Day’s Journey Into Night* works to their benefit. But here, it's just super stylized, thematically ambiguous, and incoherent in many ways.

I guess I understand what he was trying to achieve, but I feel like it was executed so badly that it just turned into a visual regurgitation of his previous works, of Diao Yinan, Akira Kurosawa, and other directors, while lacking in every other aspect.

Would like to hear your thoughts.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The first two films are two of my favourite films. Long days journey being my favourite of the two and a film I consider a masterpiece.

I think it’s pretty absurd to say resurrection is ‘unoriginal’. Some of this criticism frustrates me in the same way people roll their eyes and cry ‘self parody’ when Malick (who I love) or Wes Anderson (who I’m not as keen on) make another film in their same style and language. It’s not like cinema is flooded with Anderson or Malick films so I find the criticism that they are keeping the same style/themes as their previous films absurd. Just watch other films if you don’t like them as Directors but don’t expect them to change.

I liked Resurrection, I don’t think I loved it like I do his other two but I think that’s always a risk when you have multiple different stories in a single film. To say it’s been executed ‘badly’  is a ridiculous thing to say. He’s one of the most technically impressive and exciting new directors. Who is ambitious and original. I pray to god he keeps getting to make more films like this. I can completely understand watching the film and saying you didn’t like it, or enjoy the themes etc. But it’s clearly a very well made and impressive piece of filmmaking. It’s objectively not executed ‘badly’. 

I think you are putting something under an absurd microscope when if you pull back and look at pretty much every other film out this year - you’d realise it’s probably not deserving of this level of criticism. Your post just comes across as hipster ‘I’m very smart and this is actually bad’ contrarianism.
2. I’ll be honest, based purely upon this post I wouldn’t even be sure you watched the movie.

Resurrection is not just “Person X searching for Person Y” considering that Person X finds Person Y in the first 15 minutes of the movie. The rest of the movie is just experiencing the Deliriant’s dying dreams of film. It feels weird to base so much of your issue around the film being ‘unoriginal’ off it having a plot which it does not have.

Also is the movie really unoriginal compared to his others structurally? I haven’t seen Long Day’s Journey Into Night, but my memories of Kaili Blues weren’t of it being a semi-anthology film. From a structural perspective, Resurrection and Kaili Blues are quite heavily distinct. 

In what world does Resurrection look like Netflix pastiche? The framing, lighting, composition, staging, use of movement, coloring is frankly incomparable to average Netflix slop. Also I don’t really see your problem with the cinematography. Sure the film plays more into artifice than something like Kaili Blues. That’s because Resurrection is a meta film constructed to draw our attention to the formal implementation of the medium itself whereas this is not a concern of something like Kaili Blues. It also feels strange to me to complain so much about Resurrection being unoriginal but then go onto say the stylization throughout the film is highly different from his earlier work and worse for being different (“lacking the rawness of his earlier films”).

The narrative, plot wise, is fairly simple and not incoherent at all. There’s a world where eternal life is achieved by way of forgoing dreams. There are a few who choose to dream regardless (Deliriants). They are hunted, disconnected from their dreams, and killed. One such Deliriant dreams of films. A hunter finds him and decides to allow him to watch a final few films over a hundred year span before he eventually dies. He experiences the films and the hunter watches in an attempt to understand his draw to film. Eventually his time comes to pass. The hunter delivers him to death but offers one final film to communicate her understanding of his desire for cinema. Not that hard. How everything fits together symbolically is ambiguous, but figuring that out is part of the fun.
3. No offense but I think you might have missed a lot of the story when you watched the film. Whether or not you liked it the story was not “incoherent.” Clearly the film is about, among other things, the power of cinema for Gan. And to the degree that self-referential sections of the film that are ABOUT film to some degree, I can see turning some people off. I loved it.
4. I think there’s a major historical, and culturally specific aspect of this film that you’re overlooking. It definitely isn’t incoherent when considered through the lens of the audience it was made for.
  - Exactly this. Each of the chapters corresponds to a specific period in 20th century Chinese history (the Sino-Japanese war, the Cultural Revolution, the post-Deng economic boom, etc.), and this only deepens its satisfaction – though from their post, I’m not sure OP sensed any of that.
5. I have yet to see his other films personnally and I'm not well versed enough in film language to properly call something amateurish. It's definitively a "hit or miss" movie and I've seen a whole spectrum of opinions on it as of now.
Personnally I loved it a lot. I'm not gonna pretend I understood everything, but I was completely enthralled and hypnotized by the beautiful visuals and incredible atmosphere of each segment. I was never bored and at least for me it could have kept going for another hour and I wouldn't mind. 
The biggest positive I can say about it, that many will disagree with, is that I think it managed really well to be an hommage to cinema as a whole, where so many recent movies like Babylon completely failed to. I usually groan at those "movies about movies", but the final sequence genuinely got me emotional.
But your take is valid. When I got out of the theater I already knew this was going to be controversial lol
6. Haven’t seen Resurrection yet but a huge Bi Gan film. Always curious to read takes like this because there is a touch of “fandom” to Bi Gan’s work sometimes, eg the Stalker & Lynch homages in LDJITN. So I’m wary that Resurrection with its plot Bi might verge to much on being a fanboy rather than trying to stand shoulder to shoulder with the greats. I’ll make up my own mind when I see it, certainly there have been enough glowing reviews that I’m sure this film will have something for me.
7. I've never seen his other films and thought this was ambitious but amateurish and a bit all over the place. There were several scenes that I really loved, but otherwise the overt symbolism didn't land with me. The dreamscapes were too literal and resolving to feel convincing
8. Not sure why the comments are so hostile. I felt the same way, it is a beautiful but aimless movie. I get what he was going for with the evolution of cinema through time but most of the movie was empty and underwhelming. The final segment especially, on paper it sounds like exactly my thing but it was so hamfisted. I left feeling like I had seen some beautiful shots, but that my time had been wasted on a spectacle. Hope the next one is better!
  - Not aimless at all — the vignettes revolve around each sense identified in Buddhism (mind, sight, hearing, taste, smell, and touch) and intertwine throughout. The first viewing is definitely getting a lay of the land and the second go brings everything together. For example, second chapter (noir / sound) felt underwhelming during my first watch but really blew me away after revising. Easily one of the greatest theatre experiences I’ve ever had and people are gonna miss out dismissing it as empty!

## 2. Total Recall (1990) - How evil was Cohaagen?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Suffocating a bunch of sex-workers and some goldfish in a fit of rage was unforgivable, but is he really that bad a guy?  
  
From what I gather he was part of the terraforming of Mars, making it habitable to humans. The mutants are not native Martians, they’re humans who moved to Mars, ‘cheap domes’ made them mutate but they chose to live there. Having to buy Cohaagen’s air sucks but, again, they chose to live there, they knew the deal, and I can’t see why they couldn’t catch a flight back to Earth if they wanted an easier life.  
  
And yet… they’re violently attacking the authorities who are supplying them with air 🤷🏻‍♂️ Is it so evil to deprive these terrorists of oxygen..?  
  
The alien contraption turns out to fill the planet with oxygen, but Cohaagen doesn’t know this, it’s very possible that it could have destroyed or damaged the planet - nobody knows exactly what will happen until someone finally presses the ‘button’. Cohaagen was right to be sceptical and cautious.  
  
How evil was he?

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Seems to me the mutants were quite poor, they're portrayed as prostitutes and part of the underworld/underclass. I doubt they could afford to leave Mars, and they also wouldn't be suited to life on earth. They are forced to buy the air or they'll die.
  - Couldn’t they use the funds they’ve been spending on guns and explosives to instead by tickets back to Earth?

---
